parentQuestion: The obturator and femoral nerves branch from which plexus?

scaffolds.q: The thigh has muscles on the front (anterior) and inside (medial).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The anterior thigh has muscles for straightening the knee (quadriceps). The medial thigh has muscles for squeezing your legs together (adductors).

scaffolds.q: The lumbar region refers to the lower back.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Lumbar = lower back. You have 5 lumbar vertebrae (L1-L5). The lumbar plexus is formed from spinal nerves in this region.

scaffolds.q: The lumbar plexus is formed from spinal nerves L1-L4.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The lumbar plexus (L1-L4) supplies the lower abdomen, anterior thigh, and medial thigh. It's the electrical panel for the front of your leg.

scaffolds.q: The femoral nerve supplies the muscles that straighten (extend) the knee.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The femoral nerve innervates the quadriceps - the powerful muscles on the front of your thigh that extend your knee. Damage it, and your knee buckles.

scaffolds.q: The obturator nerve supplies the muscles that squeeze your legs together (adductors).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The obturator nerve innervates the adductor muscles on the inner thigh. These muscles bring your legs together (adduct them).

scaffolds.q: Both the femoral and obturator nerves come from L2-L4 spinal levels.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Both nerves arise from L2, L3, and L4 roots within the lumbar plexus. Same roots, different destinations - one goes anterior, one goes medial.

scaffolds.q: The lumbar plexus supplies the front and inner parts of the thigh.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Lumbar plexus nerves go to the anterior thigh (femoral) and medial thigh (obturator). The back of the thigh is supplied by the sacral plexus (sciatic nerve).

scaffolds.q: The femoral nerve passes under the inguinal ligament to reach the anterior thigh.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The femoral nerve exits the pelvis by passing under the inguinal ligament (at the groin crease). It then supplies the quadriceps and gives sensation to the anterior thigh.

scaffolds.q: Both the femoral and obturator nerves are branches of the lumbar plexus, not the sacral plexus.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The lumbar plexus (L1-L4) gives these nerves. The sacral plexus (L4-S4) is different - it gives the sciatic nerve for the back of the leg. Remember: lumbar = front of thigh, sacral = back of thigh.
